        "71046"
      ]

    "noSurprisesFlags": [
      "Patient is self-pay with no insurance listed; No Surprises Act protections do not apply to self-pay patients but excessive facility markup ($500 facility fee represents 7.2% of total) suggests potential balance billing concern if insurance were involved",
      "Emergency visit with substantial gap between charges ($6,915) and allowed amount ($1,420) indicates potential out-of-network pricing or lack of negotiated rate"
    ],
    "redFlags": [
      "DUPLICATE CPT CODE 99284: ED visit level 4 billed twice for same date of service ($2,850 x 2 = $5,700). This is the most significant charge and appears to be a duplicate entry that should be removed.",
      "POSSIBLE UPCODING: Two level-4 ED visits for routine evaluation (basic metabolic panel, venipuncture, chest x-ray) with fentanyl administration may not justify two separate high-level visit codes rather than a single code.",
      "EXCESSIVE FACILITY FEE: $500 facility fee represents 7.2% of total charges. Hospital emergency facility fees typically range 3-5% of professional fees.",
      "SELF-PAY STATUS WITH HIGH MARKUP: Large gap between billed ($6,915) and allowed ($1,420) suggests self-pay patients may be charged significantly higher rates than insured patients for identical services.",
      "FENTANYL DOSING: Two doses of Fentanyl 0.1mg ($80 each) billed separately; verify whether both were medically necessary or if this represents duplicate administration.",
      "LIMITED ALLOWED AMOUNT DETAIL: EOB shows insurance allowed of $1,420 total but does not break down which line items were reduced, making verification difficult."
    ],
    "disputeActions": [
      "PRIORITY: Call Mercy Hospital billing department immediately and request itemized explanation for the two CPT 99284 charges on 2026-04-15. Request removal of the duplicate ED visit charge ($2,850). Provide date of service and request corrected bill within 48 hours.",
      "Request itemized EOB from insurance showing allowed amounts for each CPT code individually to verify the $1,420 allowed is correctly applied and to identify which services were most heavily discounted.",
      "Request detailed facility fee justification. Challenge the $500 facility fee and ask for comparison to industry standard percentages (typically 3-5%). Request reduction or removal if unjustified.",
      "Request separate itemized bill breaking down the two Fentanyl J3010 charges with medical justification for both doses. If only one dose was administered, request credit of $80.",
      "If the duplicate ED visit charge is removed, recalculate total billed as $4,065. Request patient responsibility be recalculated proportionally if insurance allowed is adjusted accordingly.",
      "Obtain cash-pay discount request form from hospital and negotiate self-pay rate. Self-pay patients are sometimes eligible for 20-40% discounts if billed as self-pay from the start rather than after insurance denial.",
      "Request written explanation of how the insurance allowed amount of $1,420 was calculated and which services fall into that category. Verify it is not capped artificially because patient is self-pay."
    ],
    "summary": "This $6,915 emergency department bill contains a critical billing error: CPT code 99284 (ED visit level 4) is billed twice for the identical date of service (2026-04-15), inflating the bill by $2,850. When this duplicate is removed, the actual medical charges total ~$4,065. The bill also flags for excessive facility fees ($500 or 7.2% of charges, above the 3-5% industry norm) and possible upcoding of two level-4 visits for routine labs and imaging that might consolidate into a single visit. The patient is self-pay with no insurance coverage shown, yet the EOB indicates an insurance allowed amount of $1,420 against charges of $6,915—a 79.5% reduction suggesting the hospital has very different pricing for uninsured patients or the allowed amount reflects a historical insurance contract rate that no longer applies. The imaging (chest x-ray 71046 at $325) could be performed at a freestanding imaging center for ~$130–195, saving $130–195 if the patient had access to such facilities. The immediate action is disputing the duplicate ED visit charge with billing; this single correction could reduce the patient responsibility substantially. The wide gap between billed and allowed amounts also warrants investigation into whether the patient qualifies for financial assistance programs or contracted rates despite self-pay status.",
    "pricingContext": {
      "state": "NC",
      "stateMultiplier": 0.95,
      "commercialMultiplier": 2.55,
      "facilityType": "emergency_room",
      "facilityMultiplier": 2.8,

  },
  "summary_for_llm": "Total billed: $6,915.\nPatient owes: $1,420.\nInsurance paid: $0.\nFacility type: emergency room.\n6 line item(s) extracted.\n6 red flag(s) identified:\n  - DUPLICATE CPT CODE 99284: ED visit level 4 billed twice for same date of service ($2,850 x 2 = $5,700). This is the most significant charge and appears to be a duplicate entry that should be removed.\n  - POSSIBLE UPCODING: Two level-4 ED visits for routine evaluation (basic metabolic panel, venipuncture, chest x-ray) with fentanyl administration may not justify two separate high-level visit codes rather than a single code.\n  - EXCESSIVE FACILITY FEE: $500 facility fee represents 7.2% of total charges. Hospital emergency facility fees typically range 3-5% of professional fees.\n  - SELF-PAY STATUS WITH HIGH MARKUP: Large gap between billed ($6,915) and allowed ($1,420) suggests self-pay patients may be charged significantly higher rates than insured patients for identical services.\n  - FENTANYL DOSING: Two doses of Fentanyl 0.1mg ($80 each) billed separately; verify whether both were medically necessary or if this represents duplicate administration.\nDispute actions:\n  - PRIORITY: Call Mercy Hospital billing department immediately and request itemized explanation for the two CPT 99284 charges on 2026-04-15. Request removal of the duplicate ED visit charge ($2,850). Provide date of service and request corrected bill within 48 hours.\n  - Request itemized EOB from insurance showing allowed amounts for each CPT code individually to verify the $1,420 allowed is correctly applied and to identify which services were most heavily discounted.\n  - Request detailed facility fee justification. Challenge the $500 facility fee and ask for comparison to industry standard percentages (typically 3-5%). Request reduction or removal if unjustified.\n  - Request separate itemized bill breaking down the two Fentanyl J3010 charges with medical justification for both doses. If only one dose was administered, request credit of $80.\n  - If the duplicate ED visit charge is removed, recalculate total billed as $4,065. Request patient responsibility be recalculated proportionally if insurance allowed is adjusted accordingly.\n\nSummary: This $6,915 emergency department bill contains a critical billing error: CPT code 99284 (ED visit level 4) is billed twice for the identical date of service (2026-04-15), inflating the bill by $2,850. When this duplicate is removed, the actual medical charges total ~$4,065. The bill also flags for excessive facility fees ($500 or 7.2% of charges, above the 3-5% industry norm) and possible upcoding of two level-4 visits for routine labs and imaging that might consolidate into a single visit. The patient is self-pay with no insurance coverage shown, yet the EOB indicates an insurance allowed amount of $1,420 against charges of $6,915—a 79.5% reduction suggesting the hospital has very different pricing for uninsured patients or the allowed amount reflects a historical insurance contract rate that no longer applies. The imaging (chest x-ray 71046 at $325) could be performed at a freestanding imaging center for ~$130–195, saving $130–195 if the patient had access to such facilities. The immediate action is disputing the duplicate ED visit charge with billing; this single correction could reduce the patient responsibility substantially. The wide gap between billed and allowed amounts also warrants investigation into whether the patient qualifies for financial assistance programs or contracted rates despite self-pay status."
}
